agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, MHC Class I -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I is a diverse set of cell surface receptors expressed on all nucleated cells in the body and platelets. MHC is also known as human leukocyte antigen (HLA), of which there are 3 subtypes: HLA-A, HLA-B, and HLA-C. These molecules play a vital role in the immune system recognizing self from nonself, presenting foreign antigens to other immune cells. HLA class I alleles are extremely polymorphic among the world population, which presents issues relating to human tissue transplants. [1] [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Alloimmunization
agent1_snippets.content: Alloimmunization occurs when the immune system becomes sensitized to antigens not present in the host. This can lead to the production of alloantibodies against RBC-specific antigens, human leukocyte antigen (HLA) I antigens on platelets and leukocytes, HLA II antigens present on leukocytes, granulocyte-specific antigens, and platelet-specific or human platelet antigens (HPA).

agent1_snippets.title: Genetics, Human Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) -- Testing
agent1_snippets.content: 1- Sequence-specific primer (SSP) typing: This technique uses sequence-specific primer pairs that target and amplify a particular DNA sequence where PCR primers are designed to bind only to a specific HLA allele or allele groups. Confirmation of the amplified alleles is by the presence or absence of DNA amplification by a particular primer pair that are visualized by agarose gel electrophoresis . [2] [13] Sequence-specific primers used in this method are directed to specific targets. Therefore the presence of the amplification will correspond to the presence of the designated allele(s). Commercially available primer pair sets can define a full HLA type of an individual (HLA-A, -B,-C, -DR, -DQ, and -DP). Deceased donor typing is the most common application of this method as it provides rapid results. This technique allows for low-resolution HLA typing in the DNA based typing nomenclature (A*01). [2] [14] Disadvantage SSP typing is not suitable for testing a large number of samples. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Genetics, Human Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: HLA is an important barrier for hematopoietic stem cell (HPC) transplantation. Therefore, HLA matching and compatibility between the donor and the recipient is necessary for a successful HPC transplantation. HLA antigens also have a role in graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), a potentially serious complication of allogeneic stem cell transplantation. GVHD occurs when donor T cells react to host antigens on antigen-presenting cells (APCs) and attack host tissues, with sequential activation of donor T cells and monocytes/macrophages. [16]

agent1_snippets.title: Kidd Blood Group System -- Introduction -- Kidd Blood Group Antigens
agent1_snippets.content: The Kidd blood group system consists of three antigens present on RBCs—Jk a , Jk b , and Jk3. These antigens are not found on granulocytes, lymphocytes, monocytes, and platelets. Similar to other Kidd antigens, Kidd antigens are resistant to ficin, papain, trypsin, chymotrypsin, and pronase. Furthermore, the reactivity of Kidd antibodies is enhanced by these proteolytic enzymes during testing. [7] [8] [9]

agent1_snippets.title: May-Hegglin Anomaly -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: The mutated MYH9 gene is present on chromosome 22q12–13 and encodes the nonmuscle myosin heavy chain class IIA (NMMHC-IIA). [2] At least 33 mutations in the MYH9 gene have been identified. NMMHC-IIA is a cytoplasmic protein that is present in many tissues including platelets. The mutation causes abnormal production of NMMHC-IIA. This leads to macrothrombocytopenia secondary to defective megakaryocytic maturation and fragmentation. The crescent shaped Dohle-like inclusion bodies are precipitates of MHC within the cytoplasm of eosinophils, neutrophils, and monocytes. Neutrophil and platelet function is considered to be normal.

agent1_snippets.title: Genetics, Histocompatibility Antigen -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Genetically encoded molecules, known as major histocompatibility complex (MHC) molecules or human leukocyte antigens (HLA), determine an individual’s ability to produce an immune response to different antigens. These MHC molecules are responsible for determining if a molecule is histocompatible with the individual and for initiating an immune response if it is deemed foreign. [1] They are found in all nucleated cells and primarily function in antigen presentation to T cells. For an antigen presenting cell (APC) and a T cell to interact and initiate an immune response, the T cell must bind to the APC as well as the antigen bound to an MHC molecule. A major histocompatibility complex is a set of genes that code these MHC molecules/HLA antigens. Multiple genes code for different parts of the molecules, and several possible combinations of gene products exist. As a result, the chance of two individuals expressing identical MHC molecules is tremendously low. Clinically, MHC molecules are significant as they are the principal mediators involved in transplant rejection. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Pure Red Cell Aplasia -- Pathophysiology -- PRCA Associated with Lymphoproliferative Disorders
agent1_snippets.content: Researchers suggest several mechanisms whereby T-cells reduce erythropoiesis. Such mechanisms may involve T-helper-cell-mediated antibody production, major histocompatibility complex- (MHC-) restricted or unrestricted red cell progenitor recognition, or MHC-unrestricted cytotoxicity. T-cells have also been shown to downregulate the inhibitory responses of natural killer cells and cause lysis of erythroid colony-forming units.

agent1_snippets.title: Genetics, Human Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) -- Testing
agent1_snippets.content: HLA typing is defined polymorphisms of class I and class II loci. [11]  Serology was the first HLA typing methodology. The microcytoxicity method detects the presence of antigen by cytotoxic cell death induced by activation of complement. In this in vitro assay, T or B lymphocytes are isolated from an individual and are incubated with serum containing HLA antibodies of a known HLA specificity obtained from multiparous women. [12] [13] The enhancements of molecular methodology eliminate the necessity of HLA typing by using serum with anti-HLA antibodies of all HLA antigens. Although the polymorphisms of the HLA are widely distributed throughout the gene, the vast majority of the polymorphisms congregate within exons 2 and 3. In the modern day, molecular methods are used to identify the specific nucleotide sequence polymorphisms distinctive of a specific HLA antigen. Subsequently, artificially synthesized commercially available DNA probes or primers are used to detect HLA polymorphisms. [13]

agent1_snippets.content: Patients with  group II discrepancies experience an unexpected reaction in the forward grouping. These discrepancies occur in patients with weakly reacting antigens due to a low antigen burden on the RBC surface, the presence of another antigen similar to AB antigens on the RBC surface reacting with the antibody, or partial deletion of antigens on the surface of cells. agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, MHC Class I -- Related Testing -- Serological Testing
agent1_snippets.content: Serological testing generally uses a recipient's lymphocytes obtained from their sera and incubated with anti–sera-containing antibodies against various HLA class 1 subtypes. The solution is then incubated with rabbit sera, providing a source of complement; a dye is also added to identify dead cells. This assay progresses serially with different HLA antibodies put into each well of a tray. Eliminating those wells with a positive result allows for determining the HLA type. This method provides a relatively quick and easy way to determine general subtypes of HLA's present. Still, it does not offer an in-depth analysis of the true molecular identity of the HLAs. This method is less common today because of its inability to detect small changes in HLA types that may make an immunological difference and cause a transfusion reaction. [8] [10] [8]

agent2_snippets.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
agent2_snippets.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
agent2_snippets.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
agent2_snippets.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
agent2_snippets.content: The collection “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” comprises a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also members of this autoimmune group, illustrating the systemic nature of immune‑mediated disease. Although non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية) is listed among these entities, it does not result from autoantibody‑mediated red‑cell destruction; its inclusion reflects clinical overlap and underscores the importance of distinguishing immune from non‑immune mechanisms when evaluating hemolysis. Recognizing that these diverse conditions are linked by an underlying autoimmune process helps clinicians diagnose, monitor, and manage patients with immune‑driven pathology more effectively.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
agent2_snippets.content: Autoimmune disorders classified under اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ) encompass a diverse range of conditions that share an underlying immune‑mediated pathophysiology. فقر الدم الوبيل (warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia) is included in this group, reflecting the immune system’s attack on red blood cells leading to hemolysis. فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold agglutinin disease) is also a member, representing a similar hemolytic process that is triggered by cold temperatures. التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) falls within the same SMQ category, illustrating how autoimmune mechanisms can target synovial joints and cause chronic inflammation and pain. Finally, التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) is part of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), highlighting that autoimmune activity can affect vascular structures and contribute to coronary artery disease. Recognizing these entities—warm and cold autoimmune hemolytic anemias, arthritis, and coronary artery inflammation—as members of a common autoimmune disorder group underscores their shared immunological basis and supports integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
agent2_snippets.content: Aplastic anemia, arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). This categorization reflects that each of these diseases involves dysregulated immune activity that targets the body’s own tissues—whether the bone marrow in aplastic anemia, synovial joints in arthritis, red blood cells in the two forms of hemolytic anemia, or the coronary arterial wall in coronary artery inflammation. Recognizing these conditions as part of a common autoimmune spectrum highlights shared pathogenic pathways and can guide clinicians toward integrated diagnostic approaches and therapeutic strategies that address underlying immune dysfunction.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
agent2_snippets.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)) includes a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is classified as an autoimmune disease, as is non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Inflammatory joint disease (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also considered members of this autoimmune group. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying autoimmune mechanism guides clinicians toward appropriate immunomodulatory treatments and vigilant monitoring for systemic involvement.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1482
agent2_snippets.content: The classification “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses several distinct conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Within this group, التهاب المفاصل is recognized as an autoimmune form of arthritis, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد represents cold‑agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and التهاب الشريان التاجي denotes inflammation of the coronary arteries driven by autoimmune mechanisms. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is listed among these entities, it is in fact a hemolytic anemia that does not arise from autoimmunity and therefore should be distinguished from the true autoimmune members of the “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” category. Recognizing which disorders are truly autoimmune is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic targeting, and optimal patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 751
agent2_snippets.content: Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor, placing it within the product class of selective adhesion molecule inhibitors. In addition, crizanlizumab is classified as both an immunomodulatory agent (imunomodulátory) and an immunosuppressive agent (imunosupresiva), reflecting its dual capacity to modify and dampen immune system activity. These properties align crizanlizumab with the broader disposition of immunomodulators, indicating that its therapeutic action involves regulation of immune responses through targeted inhibition of adhesion molecules. This integrated classification underscores crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune pathways and its potential utility in conditions where controlling immune cell adhesion and activity is clinically beneficial.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
agent2_snippets.content: Prednison may be used to treat فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia). By suppressing the immune response, prednison reduces the production of auto‑antibodies that target red blood cells, thereby decreasing hemolysis and improving anemia. This therapeutic option is important for managing patients whose red blood cell destruction is driven by an autoimmune process.
